title: brute_force_login
description: An attacker launches a high-volume brute-force attack against the organisation's admin portal, attempting thousands of password combinations per minute.

timeline.title: High-Volume Login Failures Detected
timeline.description: WAF and SIEM alert on 8,000 login attempts against the admin portal in 10 minutes, all targeting the 'sysadmin' account.

timeline.title: Admin Account Locked
timeline.description: The targeted account is auto-locked after exceeding the failed login threshold. Attacker pivots to other accounts.

timeline.title: Attacker Adapts
timeline.description: Brute-force attempts shift to additional accounts. Rate limiting enforced at the web application firewall.

timeline.title: Attack Traffic Blocked
timeline.description: WAF rate-limiting and CAPTCHA successfully block further attempts. No successful authentication recorded.

timeline.title: Log Review Complete
timeline.description: Full review of authentication logs confirms no successful logins during the attack window.

timeline.title: Security Controls Updated
timeline.description: MFA enforced, password complexity policy strengthened, and geo-blocking applied for known attack source ranges.

node-1.title: Brute-Force Alert: Admin Portal
node-1.situation: The SIEM and web application firewall both alert on 8,000 login attempts against the admin portal in 10 minutes, all targeting a single 'sysadmin' account. The attempts are coming from a single IP and cycling through a dictionary of common passwords.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Enable IP-based rate limiting at the WAF and temporarily lock the targeted account while investigating.

options.label: B
options.text: Wait to see if the attacker succeeds before escalating the alert.

options.label: C
options.text: Change the admin password immediately and take no other action.

node-2.title: Attacker Shifts Targets
node-2.situation: The targeted account has been locked. The attacker has now shifted the brute-force attempts to other accounts in the portal, cycling through a list of known usernames. Rate limiting has slowed but not stopped the attack.
node-2.question: How do you respond?

options.label: A
options.text: Deploy CAPTCHA on the login page and enforce account lockout policies across all accounts in the portal.

options.label: B
options.text: Unlock the original account to see what password the attacker is trying.

node-3.title: Attack Halted — Log Review
node-3.situation: CAPTCHA and lockout policies have effectively halted the attack. Security indicates the attacker used a dictionary of 10,000 common passwords and none succeeded. A thorough log review is still required.
node-3.question: What do you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Review all authentication logs for the attack window to confirm no successful logins, and enforce a minimum password complexity policy for all accounts.

options.label: B
options.text: Clear the logs to free up storage space now that the attack is over.

node-4.title: No Breach Confirmed — Prevention Planning
node-4.situation: Log review confirms no accounts were successfully authenticated during the attack. Leadership wants assurance that brute-force attacks cannot succeed in the future.
node-4.question: How do you prevent future brute-force attacks?

options.label: A
options.text: Implement MFA for all admin accounts, increase lockout sensitivity, and apply geo-blocking rules for IP ranges associated with the attack.

options.label: B
options.text: Simply monitor for future attempts and only act if an account is actually compromised.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: No accounts were compromised. Rate limiting, CAPTCHA, lockout policies, and MFA have been deployed. The attack source has been geo-blocked and the incident is documented for future reference.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
